summary: Reviewed the pre-implementation timing/table spec against the included source snippets. The main risk is false confidence that changing defaults is enough: visible timing text and countdowns are hard-coded, so existing 40s sessions and any custom reveal config can be misrepresented. The host dashboard table change is broadly feasible, but the spec drops at least one currently visible discriminator and has a test-command mismatch to resolve.
appears_sound: Changing `DEFAULT_REVEAL_CONFIG` is the correct primary timing lever because `getRevealPhase` and `computeRevealState` default through it in `lib/live/reveal.ts:10`.
Leaving challenge auto-advance at 90s is consistent with the separate `CHALLENGE_REVEAL_CONFIG.nextMs: 90_000` in `lib/live/types.ts:25`.

The host dashboard actions are already isolated in handlers, so the table conversion can reuse import, delete, re-download, and brand update behavior from `app/host/page.tsx:77` through `app/host/page.tsx:224`.
Updating `lib/live/reveal.test.ts` alongside `lib/live/types.ts` is the right place to catch threshold regressions in the core reveal logic.

findings.description: The spec says existing saved sessions keep their stored 40s timing, but it plans to replace the guest countdown baseline with a hard-coded 60s value. A guest viewing an existing 40s session would see the wrong next-song time while runtime behavior follows the stored session config.
findings.evidence: `LiveSessionV1` stores per-session `revealConfig` at `lib/live/types.ts:61`, while the guest countdown uses a hard-coded `(runtime.isChallengeSong ? 90_000 : 30_000)` at `app/guest/[sessionId]/page.tsx:354`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The host could overwrite all existing session configs before guests view them, but that contradicts the spec's stated no-migration behavior.
findings.what_would_confirm: Display the countdown from the active session reveal config and test a persisted 40s session after changing the default to 60s.

findings.description: The planned placeholder text changes hard-code new default reveal times instead of deriving album/title/artist thresholds from `session.revealConfig`. Existing 40s sessions and future custom configs would show inaccurate reveal hints.
findings.evidence: The guest page hard-codes reveal text at `app/guest/[sessionId]/page.tsx:317`, `app/guest/[sessionId]/page.tsx:330`, and `app/guest/[sessionId]/page.tsx:340`, while the session carries the actual reveal thresholds at `lib/live/types.ts:61`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: If the placeholders are intended to describe only newly created default sessions, they are still rendered on a route that can open older sessions.
findings.what_would_confirm: Render those labels from the active reveal config and verify with both a new 60s session and an existing 40s session.

findings.description: The no-migration claim is unverified for imported or localStorage legacy sessions that may not have a persisted `revealConfig`. If validators backfill missing config from `DEFAULT_REVEAL_CONFIG`, changing the default would silently convert those sessions to 60s.
findings.evidence: `LiveSessionV1` requires `revealConfig` at `lib/live/types.ts:61`, and the dashboard imports/migrates saved sessions via `importLiveSessionJson` and `migrateLocalSessionsToSupabase` in `app/host/page.tsx:11`, but the validation/backfill code is not in the pack.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: Production data may already be uniformly v1 with `revealConfig` present in every saved session.
findings.what_would_confirm: Inspect the import, storage, and migration validators or run a fixture for an old session JSON without `revealConfig`.

findings.description: The table layout omits the existing Created timestamp, which is useful for distinguishing repeated session names or event dates. The spec says existing functionality should remain, but it does not decide whether this currently visible session metadata should be retained.
findings.evidence: The current card renders `Created: {new Date(session.createdAt).toLocaleString()}` at `app/host/page.tsx:287`, while the proposed table columns list only Name, Event Date, Games, Brand, and Actions.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: The timestamp may have been intentionally dropped as clutter rather than considered part of the workflow.
findings.what_would_confirm: Decide whether the table should keep Created as its own column or secondary text under the session name.

findings.description: The testing plan says to run `npm test`, but the project conventions list specific scripts such as `npm run test:e2e`, `npm run test:py`, and `npm run verify`. If `npm test` is not defined, an implementer following the spec literally may skip the intended reveal test coverage.
findings.evidence: The command list in `CLAUDE.md:13` documents `npm run test:e2e`, `npm run test:py`, and `npm run verify`, but not `npm test`.
findings.why_it_might_be_wrong: `package.json` could still define an undocumented `npm test` script that runs the Node tests.
findings.what_would_confirm: Check `package.json` or replace the spec command with the documented script that actually runs `lib/live/reveal.test.ts`.

empty_categories: No security or data-exposure risks were evident in the timing constant or table-layout plan.
No database schema change appears necessary if every persisted session truly already stores `revealConfig`.
No new dependency risk was introduced by the planned changes.
No issue found in the core reveal phase ordering; `lib/live/reveal.ts` remains config-driven once the correct config is supplied.
